moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Réti Opening, a flexible hypermodern system where we control the center with pieces rather than pawns. This move develops our knight while keeping options open—we can support the center from the flanks and avoid the heavily-analyzed tactical lines of 1.e4. From f3, our knight eyes the e5 and d4 squares, giving us flexibility to respond to Black's setup.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center, which is a natural response to our hypermodern approach. This move is more ambitious than quiet setups, and Black is ready to meet our pieces with pawn tension. We now have a choice: either support the center or create immediate complications on the queenside.

We challenge Black's central pawn and support the d4 square—a key hypermodern idea. By playing c4, we're inviting Black into a dynamic central battle while avoiding the drawish simplifications of taking on d5 immediately. This move controls d5 and the important b5-e5 diagonal, fitting perfectly into our flexible opening philosophy.

Black advances in the center rather than recapturing, pushing for space and central control. This is an aggressive choice that creates immediate tension; Black is gambling that the pawn will be useful on d4, even if it might become a target. We now have tactical opportunities on the queenside with our advanced b-pawn.

We launch the Michel Gambit, a sharp queenside counterattack that embodies the Réti's asymmetrical style. By sacrificing the b-pawn, we gain rapid piece activity and space on the queenside while Black's center pawn remains somewhat advanced and vulnerable. This move prepares Bb2 with tempo threats and keeps Black under immediate pressure rather than settling into a quiet maneuvering game.

Black blocks our b4 gambit and defends the d4 pawn by controlling c4, while also gaining space in the center. This is the natural response—accepting passive defense would be uncomfortable. However, Black's pawn on c5 now blocks the c-file and limits piece mobility, while our pieces will soon become very active supporting the center and queenside tension.
